retrieved_docs.title: Furosemide -- Adverse Effects -- Central Nervous System:
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity [22] Paresthesias Vertigo Dizziness Headache Blurred vision Xanthopsia

retrieved_docs.title: Torsemide -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity, especially with concomitant ototoxic drugs

retrieved_docs.title: Torsemide -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity occur with concomitant use of nephro or ototoxic medications. Animal studies have shown temporary ototoxicity with no permanent effect. [9] Further research is necessary for confirmation of temporary vs. permanent ototoxic effects in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: Antibiotics -- Issues of Concern -- Neurologic
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity Vestibular dysfunction Seizure Peripheral neuropathy [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Therapeutic Uses of Diuretic Agents -- Adverse Effects -- Loop Diuretic-specific Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity is a specific adverse effect of loop agents not shared by any other diuretic, and it manifests as sensory neural hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo, or dizziness. [126] Mechanism discussed further under toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Flucytosine -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Neurologic: Ataxia, hearing impairment, headache, paresthesia, parkinsonism, peripheral neuropathy, fever, vertigo, sedation, convulsions

retrieved_docs.title: Vancomycin -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity is a rare complication of vancomycin monotherapy and is common in patients receiving excessive vancomycin doses, concurrent ototoxic medications (eg, aminoglycosides, loop diuretics, antineoplastic agents), and those with underlying hearing loss conditions. Treatment should stop if patients experience signs of cytotoxicity, such as tinnitus, loss of hearing, and unbalanced movements. Vancomycin-induced ototoxicity may be irreversible for some patients. Serial auditory function testing may be beneficial in identifying early symptoms. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Tinnitus -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Caution must be exercised in evaluating tinnitus from a new drug because of the population's very high incidence of tinnitus. In a double-blind study, tinnitus may be reported as occurring in the placebo group. When anti-neoplastic drugs are used, regular hearing tests are done to watch for the onset of hearing loss or tinnitus so that the drug can be stopped if possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Vestibular System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Salicylate (aspirin) can reversibly eliminate outer hair cell electromotility to induce hearing loss. Salicylate competitively binds to the motor protein reversibly inhibiting electromotility. Other symptoms of aspirin toxicity include nausea, vomiting, tinnitus, hyperpnea, and disorientation. Other common ototoxic agents include aminoglycosides, furosemide, cisplatin, and quinines. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Hordeolum (Stye) -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Potential Adverse Effects of Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Topical antibiotics: Adverse effects of these agents include allergic reactions, contact dermatitis, and ocular irritation due to preservatives like benzalkonium chloride. Management includes switching to preservative-free formulations or alternative antibiotics and monitoring for worsening symptoms or hypersensitivity. [76]

retrieved_docs.title: Humphrey Visual Field -- Clinical Significance -- Results of Humphrey Field Analyzer in Various Clinical Scenarios
retrieved_docs.content: Toxic Optic Neuropathy: Central scotomas are the commonly found field defect in cases of ethambutol optic neuropathy. [58] Toxic optic neuropathy has also been reported with vigabatrin, an anti-epileptic medication. Around 30% of patients taking vigabatrin show bilateral concentric peripheral visual field loss, and severe cases may have tunnel vision and visual loss that might progress even after stopping the drug. [59] The visual loss due to vigabatrin is usually irreversible. [60] Other substances causing toxic optic neuropathy include tobacco, methanol, isoniazid, ethylene glycol, vincristine, cisplatin, methotrexate, carboplatin, amiodarone, cimetidine, digitalis, toluene, cyclosporine, sulfonamides, ciprofloxacin, chloramphenicol, linezolid, quinine, chloroquine, lithium, heavy metals (lead, mercury), lithium, and carbon monoxide. [58] [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Amikacin -- Adverse Effects -- Major Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity, vestibular, and auditory (4% to 6%) can occur when using high doses. The patient may complain of loss of balance and hearing loss, which can be permanent if drug therapy continues for prolonged periods. The ototoxicity is believed to be due to oxidative free radicals, which damage the hair cells in the cochlea. Many hospitals now routinely monitor hearing before, during, and after therapy in infants. Neuromuscular blockade is less common.

retrieved_docs.title: Salicylates Toxicity -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory Arrest Apnea Dyspnea Aspiration pneumonitis Deafness Tinnitus Asystole Hypotension Encephalopathy Seizures Coma

retrieved_docs.title: Tobramycin -- Toxicity -- Ototoxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity occurs from the loss of hair cells in the inner ear. [40] The damage to cranial nerve VIII (vestibulocochlear nerve) could cause hearing or balance dysfunctions. Once-daily administration of tobramycin is thought to help reduce the risk of cytotoxicity. This adverse effect is often irreversible. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Ofloxacin -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Information on toxicity with ofloxacin is limited. One case report of accidental overdosage has been described in which an adult female received 3 grams of ofloxacin intravenously over 45 minutes. The patient developed dizziness, hot and cold flushes, drowsiness, nausea, slurring of speech, and mild to moderate disorientation during the infusion. Laboratory testing detected no clinically significant changes in routine parameters in this patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Therapeutic Uses of Diuretic Agents -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Ototoxicity is a risk with the use of loop diuretics, and special precautions are necessary. Baseline auditory tests are necessary (especially when planning large bolus doses at high infusion rates), which will help evaluate on a case-by-case basis the effect of loop diuretics on auditory functions after a certain length of treatment. Periodic sequential monitoring of auditory functions is also advisable, and the possibility of switching to a different diuretic therapy should be contemplated in case of any adverse outcomes. [126]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
retrieved_docs.content: Amorolfine, ciclopirox, haloprogin and selenium sulfide are organized within the ATC hierarchy as members of “Other antifungals for topical use,” a subclass of “antifungals for topical use” that together with “antifungals for systemic use” falls under the broader category “ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE.” Ciclopirox also appears in the “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics” group, exerts a physiologic effect of decreased DNA replication, and is the active ingredient of ciclopirox 10 mg/mL medicated shampoo. Haloprogin and selenium sulfide share the therapeutic class “Dermatological Agents [TC].” Hexetidin is a member of the gynecological anti‑infective and antiseptic group. Methenamin is linked to an adverse reaction to hexamine, is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس), and is associated with the polymeric entity polymery, synonymous with polynoxylin. Hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsants include chlordantoin and nitrofurantoin; the latter is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: to regulate ocular pressure, nasal congestion, and blood pressure. Methyldopa, like other agents, is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity, highlighting the importance of allergy screening. Hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsants—including Dihydan and chlordantoin—form a distinct chemical class; although nitrofurantoin shares a hydantoin scaffold, it is used as a nitrofuran antibiotic rather than an anticonvulsant. Hydantoin derivatives are associated with fetal hydantoin syndrome, indicating teratogenic risk when used during pregnancy. Overall, the knowledge base integrates chemical classification, therapeutic class, pharmacologic mechanism, formulation excipients, and safety considerations (allergy and teratogenicity) to guide safe and effective patient care across antifungal, antiseptic, antihypertensive, and anticonvulsant therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 124
retrieved_docs.content: Amorolfine, hexetidin, ciclopirox, and haloprogin are grouped in the ATC “Other antifungals for topical use” subclass, which itself is a subcategory of the chemical families polynoxylin and selenium sulfide; haloprogin is additionally classified under the therapeutic class “Dermatological Agents [TC]” that includes selenium sulfide. Ciclopirox exerts antifungal activity by decreasing DNA replication, is the active ingredient of ciclopirox 10 mg/mL medicated shampoo, and is also listed in the ATC “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics” category, indicating use for genital‑tract fungal infections. Methenamin is a polymery linked to the polynoxylin family; it causes adverse reactions to hexamine and is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). The hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsant family comprises compounds such as dihydan, chlordantoin, and nitrofurantoin; this class is associated with fetal hydantoin syndrome (متلازمة الفنيتوين الجنينية), highlighting

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 119
retrieved_docs.content: preparations. Terbinafine appears both as a topical antifungal and as a systemic agent formulated as a 250 mg oral tablet; it is also present as an inactive ingredient within the demulcent excipient methylcellulose. Tolnaftate, butenafine and fenoprofen share hypersensitivity as a contraindication, and methoxsalen is contraindicated in infants while retaining antifungal activity. Methoxsalen, together with lactobacillus, is classified as a microbial‑agent‑containing product; methoxsalen represents a specific instance of this class. The “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics” ATC group contains hexetidin, chlordantoin, nifuratel, povidone‑iodine, lactobacillus and methylrosaniline (which also belongs to the topical antifungal subclass). Chlordantoin is additionally a member of the hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsant family, which includes dihydan and nitrofurantoin; nitrofurantoin is associated with a fetal syndrome described in Arabic as “متلازمة الفنيتوين الجنينية.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 45
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base organizes dermatological antifungal agents into topical and systemic categories within the ATC framework. The top‑level group “ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE” comprises the subgroups “antifungal antibiotics, topical” and “other antifungals for topical use in ATC,” which together form “antifungals for topical use,” alongside “antifungals for systemic use.” Specific agents listed under “other antifungals for topical use in ATC” include amorolfine, haloprogin, tavaborole, butenafine, terbinafin, tolnaftát and fenoprofen; haloprogin and selenium sulfide are also classified under the therapeutic class “Dermatological Agents [TC].” Polymery is mapped to polynoxylin, and both polymery and the “other antifungals for topical use in ATC” group are linked to polynoxylin as a higher‑order category. Safety considerations highlight that methenamin, the causative agent of hexamine‑related adverse reactions, is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge base organizes antifungal agents, gynecological antiseptics, anticonvulsants, and centrally acting anti‑adrenergic drugs within ATC‑based therapeutic hierarchies and highlights key safety issues. Amorolfine, ciclopirox, haloprogin, selenium sulfide, methylrosaniline, tavaborole, butenafine, terbinafine (both topical and systemic) and tolnaftát are classified in the ATC subgroup “Other antifungals for topical use,” which together with systemic antifungals forms the umbrella “Antifungals for dermatological use.” Ciclopirox and methylrosaniline also belong to the ATC group “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics,” the latter group also containing lactobacillus, hexetidin, chlordantoin and the specific agent nifuratel. Haloprogin and selenium sulfide are assigned the therapeutic class “Dermatological Agents [TC].” Tavaborole is chemically linked to the heterocyclic bicyclic compound class. Methylcelulosa serves as a parent compound for demulcent preparations and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 120
retrieved_docs.content: Other antifungals for topical use (ATC subclass) form a specific subset of the broader category of antifungals for dermatological use, which also includes systemic antifungals. Within this topical group, amorolfine, ciclopirox and haloprogin are listed, and the class overlaps chemically with polynoxylin and selenium sulfide. Ciclopirox, which also belongs to the ATC class “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics,” exerts a physiologic effect of decreased DNA replication and is the active ingredient of ciclopirox 10 mg/mL medicated shampoo. Haloprogin is classified under Dermatological Agents [TC] and is likewise associated with selenium sulfide. Hexamine‑related adverse reactions are attributed to methenamin, a specific type of the polymeric entity polymery; methenamin is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Hexetidin is another member of the “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics” class. In the anticonvulsant domain, Dihydan and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: fenoprofen (a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug) and the topical antifungals butenafine and tolnaftát are all contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). This integrated framework demonstrates the multiple therapeutic indications of these agents (dermatologic, gynecologic, anticonvulsant, antihypertensive) and underscores important safety considerations such as hypersensitivity reactions and teratogenicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 841
retrieved_docs.content: Amorolfine, haloprogin, selenium sulfide, tavaborole, butenafine and tolnaftát are all classified as members of the “Other antifungals for topical use in ATC” group. Within this therapeutic landscape, haloprogin and selenium sulfide are also identified as agents that belong to the broader therapeutic class “Dermatological Agents [TC]”, indicating their use in skin‑related conditions. The chemical category “bicyklické sloučeniny heterocyklické” is mapped to tavaborole, reflecting that tavaborole is a heterocyclic bicyclic compound used as a topical antifungal. Clinical safety considerations note that both butenafine and tolnaftát are contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس), underscoring the need to avoid these agents in individuals with allergic reactions. Together, these relationships delineate the classification, chemical nature, therapeutic context, and safety precautions for a set of topical antifungal medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 687
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa is identified as a centrally acting antiadrenergic agent and is a member of the class of centrally acting antiadrenergic agents, which in turn are a subclass of antiadrenergic agents that act on the central nervous system. Both Methyldopa and the antifungal agents butenafine and tolnaftate are contraindicated in patients who have hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). This underscores the clinical need to assess for allergic reactions before prescribing Methyldopa, butenafine, or tolnaftate, as each of these drugs should be avoided in individuals with a known hypersensitivity to the respective medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1508
retrieved_docs.content: Other antifungals for topical use in ATC is a pharmacological class that comprises the agents butenafine and tolnaftát. Both butenafine and tolnaftát are topical antifungal medications, and each is contraindicated in patients with the hypersensitivity condition described as فرط تحسس. Recognizing that these two drugs share the same contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: hydantoin‑derived agents are organized across dermatological, gynecological and systemic therapeutic contexts, highlighting overlapping uses (e.g., haloprogin, selenium sulfide, and terbinafine) and important safety considerations such as the fetal hydantoin syndrome risk associated with hydantoin derivatives.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 92
retrieved_docs.content: Butenafine and tolnaftate, like ciclopirox, carry a contraindication for patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). The “Other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics” group encompasses hexetidin, iodinated povidone, nifuratel, methylrosaniline (which also belongs to the topical antifungal subclass), and the hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsant chlordantoin. Hydantoin‑derived agents such as chlordantoin and nitrofurantoin are associated with fetal hydantoin syndrome, indicating a teratogenic risk. Antiseptics and disinfectants form an overarching class that includes subclasses such as biguanides and amidines, boric‑acid‑based antiseptics, nitrofuran derivatives, other antiseptics, phenol and its derivatives, silver compounds, and quaternary ammonium compounds. Boric acid is the active ingredient of the “Boric acid antiseptics and disinfectants” entry and also serves as an inactive ingredient in fluocinolone acetonide 0.1 MG/ML Medicated Shampoo. Silver nitrate belongs to both the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 475
retrieved_docs.content: Haloprogin, selenium sulfide, tavaborole, butenafine, and tolnaftát are all grouped within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “Other antifungals for topical use,” and they belong to the broader therapeutic class of dermatological agents. The heterocyclic chemical group “bicyklické sloučeniny heterocyklické” is mapped to tavaborole, indicating a structural relationship between this class and the antifungal agent. Both butenafine and tolnaftát are contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس), underscoring the importance of avoiding these drugs in individuals with allergic reactions. This integrated classification and contraindication information helps clinicians select appropriate topical antifungal therapies while accounting for patient‑specific allergy risks.

retrieved_docs.content: therapeutic class “Dermatological Agents [TC].” Tavaborole is chemically linked to the heterocyclic bicyclic compound class. Methylcelulosa serves as a parent compound for demulcent preparations and includes the inactive ingredient terbinafine 250 mg oral tablet, connecting it to the systemic antifungal terbinafine, which is marketed as a 250 mg oral tablet product. In the anticonvulsant domain, chlordantoin and the related hydantoin‑derivative class (including dihydan and nitrofurantoin) are associated with fetal hydantoin syndrome, indicating a teratogenic risk. Imidazoline‑receptor agonists form a subclass of agonists; moxonidine and rilmenidine are listed as products that act through imidazoline receptors and are also categorized as centrally acting anti‑adrenergic agents. Methyldopa, fenoprofen (a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug) and the topical antifungals butenafine and tolnaftát are all contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). This integrated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC classification places antifungals for dermatological use into two major groups—antifungals for topical use and antifungals for systemic use. Within the subclass “other antifungals for topical use” the agents amorolfine, ciclopirox, haloprogin, methylrosaniline, tavaborole, terbinafine and tolnaftát are listed. Ciclopirox is also categorized under “other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics”; it inhibits DNA replication and is the active ingredient of a 10 mg/mL medicated shampoo, giving it dual utility as a dermatologic antifungal and a gynecologic anti‑infective. Haloprogin belongs to the therapeutic class of dermatological agents and is associated with selenium sulfide, which itself is a member of the other topical antifungal group. Hexetidin, chlordantoin, methylrosaniline, iodinated povidone (jodovaný povidon) and nifuratel are also members of the other gynecological anti‑infectives and antiseptics. Chlordantoin is a hydantoin‑derived anticonvulsant; the hydantoin‑derivative class, which includes nitrofurantoin, is linked to fetal phenytoin syndrome, highlighting the need for caution during pregnancy. Methylrosaniline, tavaborole, terbinafine and tolnaftát share the same ATC group “other antifungals for topical use”. Tavaborole’s chemical structure is classified as a heterocyclic bicyclic compound. Terbinafine additionally belongs to the broader “antifungals for systemic use” category and is formulated as a 250 mg oral tablet for treatment of deep or disseminated fungal infections, while also being available as a topical agent. These overlapping classifications illustrate the interconnected therapeutic roles of specific antifungal agents in dermatology and gynecology, the chemical identities that define them, and the safety considerations associated with hydantoin‑derived medications.
